message.content:
CONFIGURED-panel review (one of 2; return verdict JSON, write NOTHING). Round 1.
Task: T110 (goals:G6) — "Ship the NetworkManager unmanaged-devices drop-in + install.md NM section (C1 + Q40 artifact)".
acceptance: The drop-in file exists under packaging/ with valid NM keyfile syntax; a lightweight packaging test/CI check asserts the file's presence and the unmanaged-devices key; install.md §4 gains an NM subsection referencing the shipped file (not a hand-typed inline recipe). Relates D39 in acceptance only.
Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a553d90c5a5d0afc1 (branch implement/T110, base 8d8fa77, HEAD e37dfcc). Go repo — gate via `nix develop -c sh -c '...'`.
Worker: pass; files docs/install.md, internal/config/packaging_test.go (TestNetworkManagerDropIn), packaging/networkmanager/99-wanbond-unmanaged.conf.
JUDGE: (a) is the .conf file valid NM keyfile syntax with the EXACT key `unmanaged-devices=interface-name:wanbond0` under `[keyfile]`? (b) does the packaging test genuinely assert the file's presence AND the key (read it, confirm it would fail if the key were absent/renamed — is the repo-root-relative path resolution correct so the test runs from the package dir)? (c) does install.md §4 reference the SHIPPED file (copy destination /etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/, nmcli reload/verify) rather than an inline hand-typed recipe? (d) run the full gate `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` yourself and confirm green. Return { taskId, verdict, criticism: [], questions: [], defects: [], rationale, summary }.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.description: Read conf file and packaging test

content.content:
diff --git a/docs/install.md b/docs/install.md
index a86d203..efa2d94 100644
--- a/docs/install.md
+++ b/docs/install.md
@@ -642,6 +642,40 @@ systemctl enable --now wanbond-edge      # or wanbond-concentrator
   standard hardening. If you set `wireguard.listen_port` below 1024, add
   `CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICE` to the bounding set.
 
+### NetworkManager unmanaged-devices drop-in
+
+On systems running **NetworkManager** (the default on RPi OS, Debian, and Ubuntu
+desktop), NetworkManager monitors the `wanbond0` interface for link changes and
+automatically flushes any configured IP addresses on link-up, destroying the
+tunnel routing without operator action. This failure mode (D39, D5) is prevented
+by marking `wanbond0` as an unmanaged device in NetworkManager's configuration.
+
+Deploy the shipped drop-in:
+
+```sh
+cp packaging/networkmanager/99-wanbond-unmanaged.conf /etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/
+```
+
+Then reload NetworkManager to apply the change:
+
+```sh
+nmcli general reload
+# or
+sudo systemctl reload NetworkManager
+```
+
+Verify the interface is marked unmanaged:
+
+```sh
+nmcli device show wanbond0 | grep STATE
+```
+
+The output should show `STATE: unmanaged`.
+
+If your edge box runs **systemd-networkd** instead (or alongside NetworkManager),
+you do not need this drop-in — skip this subsection and proceed to "Interface
+addressing and routing" below.
+
 ### Interface addressing and routing (operator-owned)
 
 The daemon creates the TUN interface (`wanbond0`) and brings it

+On systems running **NetworkManager** (the default on RPi OS, Debian, and Ubuntu

+

 The daemon creates the TUN interface (`wanbond0`) and brings it

content.text:
All four acceptance clauses verified against the actual diff and a self-run gate.

### Session summary
- **Did:** reviewed task T110 round 1 (diff 8d8fa77..HEAD)
- **Achieved:** verdict approve; 0 criticisms / 0 questions
- **Discovered:** (a) `packaging/networkmanager/99-wanbond-unmanaged.conf` is exactly `[keyfile]\nunmanaged-devices=interface-name:wanbond0\n` (verified via `od -c`) — valid NM keyfile syntax, exact key. (b) `TestNetworkManagerDropIn` resolves repo root by walking up from `os.Getwd()` (`internal/config`) to `go.mod`, opens the file, and requires a line containing both `unmanaged-devices` and `interface-name:wanbond0` — fails if key absent/renamed; passes now. (c) install.md §4 adds an NM subsection that `cp`s the shipped file to `/etc/NetworkManager/conf.d/` with `nmcli general reload` + `nmcli device show wanbond0` verify — references the shipped artifact, not an inline recipe. (d) full gate green: `go build ./...` BUILD_OK, `go vet ./...` VET_OK, `go test ./...` all packages ok.
- **Issues:** none. (Minor non-blocking note: the test matches the two substrings independently on a line rather than the exact full key, so a semantically-inverted `except:` form would also match; the shipped file is correct, so no impact on acceptance.)

```json
{
  "taskId": "T110",
  "verdict": "approve",
  "criticism": [],
  "questions": [],
  "defects": [],
  "rationale": "Conf file byte-verified as valid [keyfile] drop-in with the exact unmanaged-devices=interface-name:wanbond0 key; TestNetworkManagerDropIn asserts both presence and key with correct repo-root path resolution; install.md §4 references the shipped file (cp + nmcli reload/verify), not an inline recipe; self-run go build/vet/test all green.",
  "summary": "T110 approved: NM drop-in exact-key valid, packaging test asserts presence+key and passes, install.md §4 references shipped file, full gate green."
}
```
